title: Breckvale
html:
The road east is a dirt track with grass growing up the center. It is wide enough for a cart and has been used recently enough that the ruts are still clean-edged. Ian’s legs know it. They navigate a root crossing and a shallow ditch without Alden having to look down, the body carrying information the mind no longer owns.

He is hungry in a specific, advanced way that is past the first stage of it. The emaciation was already there before he arrived and the night in the forest cost more on top of it. His stomach has stopped making noise about it, which is the less reassuring version of hunger.

‘Sell the cinnamon first.’

It is the only thing he has with any trade value and he doesn’t know what it’s worth here, which means his first task in Breckvale is establishing a price before committing to a buyer. He has done this before with technical contracts and the principle is the same: talk to three people before agreeing to anything.

The forest thins over the next kilometer. The canopy breaks in pieces, morning light coming through in broader sections, and then the trees step back from both sides of the track and fields open up. The crops are already harvested, the rows turned and dark, a farmhouse visible at the far end with smoke rising from it. The road widens past the field boundary and the ruts deepen where cart traffic concentrates.

The hunger sharpens when the smoke reaches him. He doesn’t stop walking.

Breckvale appears as a roofline first, the higher buildings catching the morning sun above a low stone wall that runs the width of the road. A gate stands open, an iron bar across two stone posts, a single guard leaning against the left post with the posture of someone who has been awake since before it was worth being awake. He looks up when Alden is twenty meters out.

“Card.”

Alden hands it over. The guard reads it, looks at him once, reads it again.

“Greywood Forest.”

“Yes.”

“Long walk for someone your size.” He taps the affiliation field with one finger. “Unaffiliated. You here for assessment?”

“Assessment was last year,” Alden says. “Here for work.”

The guard looks at him the way people look at 14-year-olds who say they are here for work, with the particular flatness of someone who has heard a version of it before and found it uninteresting every time. He hands the card back.

“Market’s on Creel Street. Don’t loiter near the Assayer’s Hall without business.”

Alden pockets the card and walks through.

The town is denser than the road suggested. Buildings run two and three storeys on the main street, the upper floors overhanging the lower ones enough to narrow the sky to a strip. The stonework is old and competent, the kind that has been repaired enough times it no longer looks like any one era. A shrine to the sun deity occupies a recessed alcove across from the gate, a small carved disc mounted above a shelf where someone has left a dish of dried grain. The offering is fresh. Someone passed this morning.

He notes the Assayer’s Hall by its sign before he has to look for it. A two-storey building on the main road, a balanced scale etched into the stone lintel above the door, and below it a mounted board with several papers pinned to it. He crosses to read them without going inside.

Most are commercial: grain weighing, debt arbitration, cargo inspection. Two are pathway-related. The first is a notice of affiliation intake from the Ironthread Guild, open to assessed individuals, Agi 12 or above required, applications closed for the current quarter. The second is a general advisory from the High Council of Aldenmoor, dated six months prior, reminding unaffiliated residents that practicing any pathway ability without registered affiliation carries a penalty of confiscation and civil detention.

‘Good to know.’

He reads the advisory twice. It implies that detecting an unaffiliated practitioner is either possible or practical enough to be worth legislating, which means someone in the civil structure has a method for it. He files that and moves on.

Creel Street is one block south. The market is smaller than the main road implied, twenty stalls in two rows with a gap down the center for foot traffic. It smells of damp wool, something frying further in, and the sharp mineral note of freshly cut stone from somewhere nearby. Half the stalls are spice and dry goods. He checks two of them before stopping at a third.

The woman behind the counter is sorting small cloth packets into a wooden tray. She is somewhere past fifty with the kind of stillness that comes from doing the same work for a long time.

“Cinnamon,” Alden says, and sets the jar on the counter.

She picks it up without any change in expression, uncorks it and leans the opening toward her nose.

“Where’d you get this?”

“Family stock. I need the coin more than the cinnamon.”

She weighs the jar in her palm. “It’s decent. Smells clean. Three marks.”

‘Check the other stall first.’

“I’ll come back,” he says.

She sets the jar down and goes back to her packets. “Four if you come back before midday.”

He takes the jar and goes to the stall two rows over. That vendor looks at the cinnamon for a shorter time, quotes two marks and a half, and loses interest when Alden doesn’t immediately agree. He checks the third stall, a general dry goods seller who isn’t interested at any price because he already has cinnamon. Alden goes back to the first woman at five minutes to midday.

“Four,” she says, before he puts the jar down.

“Four and a meal recommendation.”

She looks at him for a moment. “Crowner’s, end of the street. Order the grey broth, not the stew.” She counts four marks onto the counter.

***

Crowner’s is a low-ceilinged room with long tables and a serving window at the back. The grey broth arrives in a clay bowl with a thick slice of bread across the top and costs one mark. He eats slowly because Ian’s stomach is the kind of empty where eating fast causes a specific kind of regret, and because the room is worth observing.

The other customers are a mix of tradespeople and a table of three he pegs as affiliated within the first two minutes. They wear their class badges at the collar, small enameled pins, and they carry the particular ease of people who know the local rules well enough to not think about them. Two are from a guild he doesn’t recognize, silver thread worked into a dark backing. The third wears a sun disc, the same symbol as the shrine at the gate, and has the look of someone who considers the other two a minor social obligation.

They are talking about a job. He gets the outline of it without trying: a warding contract on a grain warehouse east of town, something in the walls that the guild already assessed once and apparently did not resolve. The sun disc man thinks the first assessment was wrong. The other two do not think so but are too professionally cautious to say it directly.

He finishes the broth and keeps the bread for later.

Three marks left. It is enough for one night’s lodging at the cheaper end, or two more meals, or some combination that leaves him with nothing by tomorrow morning. He needs income before the money runs out, which is a timeline of roughly a day.

‘What can a 14-year-old unaffiliated nobody do for money.’

Labor is the obvious answer, the kind that doesn’t require a class badge or references. He has Ian’s body for the physical work, which is not the best instrument available given the current state of it, but functional. The problem is that most labor in a market town is either skilled or contracted through guilds, and an unaffiliated kid walking in off a forest road carries a specific set of assumptions he will have to work around.

He goes back to the notice board outside the Assayer’s Hall.

There are more papers on the lower section he’d skimmed past earlier. He reads them properly this time. Two requests for day labor at a tannery on the south edge of town, unskilled, payment on completion. A request for a courier run to a settlement called Mossgate, half a day northeast, one mark fifty on delivery of a sealed package. A handwritten notice for a shop assistant at a bookseller, three marks a week, must read.

He can read. In two languages, currently.

The bookseller is the only one that doesn’t solve today and create a bigger problem tomorrow. Day labor at the tannery pays once and leaves him in the same position tomorrow. The courier run solves today and nothing else. The bookseller position is the only option that compounds, three marks a week building toward something he can plan around, and inside a book shop he has access to information he currently lacks.

He writes down the address from the notice on the inside of his left hand with a fingernail, just the street name and number, and heads to find it.

Aldric’s Books is on a side street off the main road, narrow frontage between a cobbler and a building with no sign. The window display is three books propped open to middle pages and a hand-lettered price card that has been rained on. The door sticks when he pushes it. Clunk!

The smell inside is specific: old paper, dust, and something waxy from the lamp burning on the counter despite the morning light. The shelves go floor to ceiling on every wall and there is a second row of shelves down the center, a narrow passage between them. The counter is at the back.

The man behind it is perhaps sixty, with reading glasses pushed up onto his forehead and ink on three fingers. He looks at Alden the way people in this town consistently look at Alden, with the mild, patient suspicion reserved for 14-year-olds from the forest.

“Notice board,” Alden says. “Shop assistant. I can read.”

“In what language?”

“Aldenmoor common. And I can identify topics by title well enough to shelve without asking every time.”

The man tilts his head slightly. “Aldenmoor common and what else?”

“I can work through a second script slowly. Good enough for catalogue work.” He keeps Ian’s face neutral because the second script is whatever his old world’s alphabet looks like in this body, which he has no way to name without raising further questions.

The man studies him for a moment. “You’re from Greywood.” It isn’t a question.

“Yes.”

“Ian.”

Alden goes still for a half second. “Yes.”

“I knew your father,” the man says, in a tone that closes the subject as it opens it. He takes his glasses off his forehead and sets them on the counter. “Three marks a week. You sort, you shelve, you don’t bend spines. The position is six days, seventh day off. You start when you walk through that door tomorrow morning.”

“Today,” Alden says.

The man looks at him.

“I can start today. I have nothing else on.”

A short pause. “Fine. Today. There’s a delivery crate in the back room. Sort by subject, alphabetical within subject, set aside anything you can’t place and I’ll classify it. Don’t touch the locked cabinet.”

“What’s in it?”

“Things you don’t touch,” the man says, and picks up his glasses again.

Alden goes to find the back room.

The delivery crate has forty-one books in it. He works through them with his back against the wall, reading each title and a page from the middle to establish subject, setting them in piles on the floor around him. Most are practical: two on animal husbandry, one surveying manual, a collection of regional law going back four provincial administrations. Several on trade routes and seasonal markets. One slim volume on herbs and their ritual applications, placed on the floor by itself because he isn’t certain which pile it belongs to yet.

At the bottom of the crate, wrapped in cloth and tied with cord, is a book with no title on the spine. He turns it to the front cover. Blank. He unties the cord and opens it to the first page.

The page is blank too. So is the next one. He fans through the whole book: ninety or so pages, all of them empty.

‘Why would you ship an empty book.’

He wraps it back in its cloth, ties the cord, and sets it on top of the uncertain pile. That one is definitely a question for someone else.

The man appears in the doorway at some point in the afternoon. He looks at the sorted piles, the near-empty crate, and the two books in the uncertain group.

“Herbs and ritual applications goes under Practical Arts,” he says, without Alden having to ask. His eyes go to the wrapped book and stay there for a moment. “That one I’ll deal with.” He picks it up, and doesn’t explain anything further.

He pays Alden half a mark for the afternoon’s work when the light starts to go, points him toward a rooming house two streets over that charges one mark a night and doesn’t ask questions, and locks the front door while Alden is still putting on his jacket.

Outside, the street is getting dark. The shrine at the main road has a small flame lit in front of the carved disc now, recently placed. Two blocks away a bell rings the evening hour from somewhere.

Alden has two and a half marks, a job starting tomorrow morning, and a locked cabinet he has already decided to understand before the end of the week. He walks toward the rooming house.

‘The book knew who owned it.’

He isn’t sure why he thinks that. He files it anyway.
